content.assessment: A pilot approach interacts cautiously with existing institutions by not committing to national-scale change against the centralized admissions system (F-live-hungarian_education_system-03) or the 85% selective-school baseline (F-live-hungarian_education_system-01), instead testing an opt-in comprehensive model. This has strong institutional fit as an evaluation mechanism but limited system coverage by design, since it is explicitly a bounded regional test. It appropriately recognizes, in its own design, that geographic and choice-driven sorting (F-live-hungarian_education_system-06) could undermine a de-tracking pilot unless paired with zoning controls, which is institutionally sound reasoning but adds administrative complexity. The maintainer exemption structure (F-live-hungarian_education_system-05) is not addressed within the pilot's stated scope, meaning even a successful pilot would not test compatibility with the segregation channel tied to church/private school autonomy.
opportunities: Could generate Hungary-specific institutional evidence distinguishing selection-mechanism effects from confounding geographic and choice factors before any national administrative commitment
strengths: Institutionally cautious design avoids premature disruption of national admissions governance while generating comparative evidence
Explicitly anticipates that de-tracking without zoning/choice controls could fail against geographic sorting mechanisms already documented
threats: Non-representative volunteer pilot regions limit generalizability to the national administrative and demographic contexts documented in the evidence, particularly rural vocational-track funneling
weaknesses: Bounded pilot scope means system coverage of national-level selection statistics (85% selective schools) is not addressed even if successful
Maintainer exemption-based segregation is outside the pilot's design scope entirely
